bins.name: States over federal jurisdiction
bins.desc: NARUC argues state commissions have the authority and expertise to manage large load interconnection and cost allocation, and that federal regulation under the ANOPR is unnecessary and would impede states' ability to adapt. It contends no ANOPR action is needed and that changing the regime now would create uncertainty and unwind state initiatives already in place.
quotes: The abundance of recent state undertakings demonstrate that state commissions are in the best position to ensure rational and efficient interconnections of new large loads while protecting all customers (including residential, commercial and large manufacturing customers) from improper cost-shifts or unfair interconnection processes.
Federal regulation of large load interconnections will impede states’ abilities to (i) adapt quickly to industry

State commissions have the authority, regulatory tools, and unique expertise necessary to allocate transmission costs associated with large load interconnections directly to those end-use customers.  There is no regulatory gap.

NARUC also recognizes and supports the general conclusions of the Concentric Energy Advisors Whitepaper recently submitted into the record by WIRES, which highlights the potentially significant negative cost consequences to other retail customers of federal pre-emption of the large load interconnection process.
NARUC highlights six examples how the states are effectively handling large load customers and the surrounding issues:

All these examples demonstrate that states can be nimble in addressing large load interconnection issues.  By necessity, processes within RTOs, relying as they do on stakeholder consultation, are far less expeditious.
changing the regulatory regime now will result in further uncertainty in the interconnection process and potentially unwind the state initiatives already put in place, such as those described above.
no action in the ANOPR is necessary to enable state commission to cost-allocate transmission between end-use customers.

bins.name: No gap in network upgrade allocation
bins.desc: NARUC asserts states already have the tools to allocate transmission and network upgrade costs directly to large load end-use customers, so there is no regulatory gap. It warns that a federal upgrade-cost process could exempt large loads that paid through federal interconnection and shift costs to other retail ratepayers.
quotes: State commissions have the authority, regulatory tools, and unique expertise necessary to allocate transmission costs associated with large load interconnections directly to those end-use customers.  There is no regulatory gap.

it remains unclear how cost allocation would work if some large loads have already paid for network upgrades through a federal interconnection process, making them exempt from other transmission costs at the retail level.  This could lead to costs being shifted to other retail ratepayers, while limiting the states’ ability to appropriately allocate costs and protect other customers.
no action in the ANOPR is necessary to enable state commission to cost-allocate transmission between end-use customers.

bins.name: Protecting other retail customers
bins.desc: NARUC emphasizes shielding residential, commercial, and manufacturing customers from improper cost shifts and discriminatory procedures. It points to state tariff protections such as collateral, demand charges, and stranded-cost safeguards as evidence states already protect non-large-load customers.
quotes: The abundance of recent state undertakings demonstrate that state commissions are in the best position to ensure rational and efficient interconnections of new large loads while protecting all customers (including residential, commercial and large manufacturing customers) from improper cost-shifts or unfair interconnection processes.

If FERC issues different federal rules for interconnection of data center loads, this may lead to increased costs or discriminatory procedures for other important end-use customers, such as Virginia manufacturers developing critical components

it remains unclear how cost allocation would work if some large loads have already paid for network upgrades through a federal interconnection process, making them exempt from other transmission costs at the retail level.  This could lead to costs being shifted to other retail ratepayers, while limiting the states’ ability to appropriately allocate costs and protect other customers.

this new large load tariff requires a minimum contract term of 14 years, a maximum ramp period of four years, minimum collateral requirements, and minimum demand charges based on contracted capacity.  All these protections will ensure that costs are not shifted to non-large load customers.
These terms ensure large loads pay their share even if they use less power than planned and prevent potential stranded asset costs from being passed to other retail ratepayers.

bins.name: State deposit and collateral terms
bins.desc: NARUC cites state-approved collateral, deposit, and sliding-scale fee structures, such as Virginia's collateral requirements and Illinois's study deposits, as effective tools that discourage speculative requests and protect ratepayers from stranded costs.
quotes: this new large load tariff requires a minimum contract term of 14 years, a maximum ramp period of four years, minimum collateral requirements, and minimum demand charges based on contracted capacity.  All these protections will ensure that costs are not shifted to non-large load customers.

This sliding-scale approach provides appropriate incentives for customers to submit realistic and supportable demand forecasts and helps mitigate the risk of speculative or inflated requests that could lead to inefficient planning or unnecessary system investment.

bins.name: States act faster than RTOs and FERC
bins.desc: NARUC argues states can act more nimbly and expeditiously than RTO stakeholder processes or Section 206 FERC proceedings, which lack the statutory deadlines state commissions face.
quotes: All these examples demonstrate that states can be nimble in addressing large load interconnection issues.  By necessity, processes within RTOs, relying as they do on stakeholder consultation, are far less expeditious.

bins.name: Cost allocation to large loads
bins.desc: NARUC supports allocating large load interconnection and transmission costs to the large load customers themselves and preventing cost shifts to other ratepayers. It cites state tariffs using minimum demand charges, take-or-pay terms, exit fees, and collateral to achieve this, and warns federal preemption could impose negative cost consequences on other customers.
quotes: The abundance of recent state undertakings demonstrate that state commissions are in the best position to ensure rational and efficient interconnections of new large loads while protecting all customers (including residential, commercial and large manufacturing customers) from improper cost-shifts or unfair interconnection processes.

State commissions have the authority, regulatory tools, and unique expertise necessary to allocate transmission costs associated with large load interconnections directly to those end-use customers.  There is no regulatory gap.

NARUC also recognizes and supports the general conclusions of the Concentric Energy Advisors Whitepaper recently submitted into the record by WIRES, which highlights the potentially significant negative cost consequences to other retail customers of federal pre-emption of the large load interconnection process.

If FERC issues different federal rules for interconnection of data center loads, this may lead to increased costs or discriminatory procedures for other important end-use customers, such as Virginia manufacturers developing critical components

it remains unclear how cost allocation would work if some large loads have already paid for network upgrades through a federal interconnection process, making them exempt from other transmission costs at the retail level.  This could lead to costs being shifted to other retail ratepayers, while limiting the states’ ability to appropriately allocate costs and protect other customers.

this new large load tariff requires a minimum contract term of 14 years, a maximum ramp period of four years, minimum collateral requirements, and minimum demand charges based on contracted capacity.  All these protections will ensure that costs are not shifted to non-large load customers.
These terms ensure large loads pay their share even if they use less power than planned and prevent potential stranded asset costs from being passed to other retail ratepayers.

This sliding-scale approach provides appropriate incentives for customers to submit realistic and supportable demand forecasts and helps mitigate the risk of speculative or inflated requests that could lead to inefficient planning or unnecessary system investment.
no action in the ANOPR is necessary to enable state commission to cost-allocate transmission between end-use customers.
